what_it_does: This file is a read-only, exhaustive landscape inventory of the `ops` area and adjacent platforms/docs/skills/contexts for the Company system. It enumerates the `company` CLI surface, the service registry, ops runbooks, helper scripts, systemd units/timers, platforms, docs, skills, contexts, operational workflows, and a curated list of known gaps/stale items, explicitly aiming for volume and refusing to filter or judge entries away.
observations: The `company` CLI in `ops/cli/app.py` is described as the single control surface for the whole running system, dispatching commands for status, GPU, health, suites, coherence, models, telemetry, sessions, board, clone, combos, config, swap, ensure, up/down/restart, and logs.

`ops/services.json` is treated as the single self-describing source of truth for every service, listing groups (core/brain/voice/models/reach/jobs), ports, systemd units, VRAM estimates, combos, and notable gaps like shared ports and manual services.
`ops/cli/gpu.py` is called the ONE VRAM authority, used by both the CLI and `voice/lifecycle.py`, with eviction priority models→brain→voice and orphan-safe teardown.
The registry reader `ops/cli/registry.py` is stdlib-only and provides target resolution, VRAM lookup, combo listing, config editing, and shared-port detection.

A capability catalog in `ops/model_capabilities.json` is loaded by `ops/cli/capabilities.py` at import with fail-loud validation; it joins model-id capability tags to service deployment/VRAM/resident status.
Ops runbooks (`ops/AGENTS.md`, `ops/BOOT-RUNBOOK.md`, `ops/STARTUP.md`, `ops/WINDOWS-BOOT.md`) are summarized, with `BOOT-RUNBOOK.md` flagged as authoritative and `WINDOWS-BOOT.md` flagged stale.
Numerous non-CLI Python scripts are catalogued, including transcript exporters/importers, reindex beats, dragnet CLIs, rerank server, fabric live probes, commit queue, and self-seeding helpers.

Systemd units in `ops/systemd/` are listed, including company-owned units, third-party vLLM/voice units, and generated routine units; several voice trial engine units are noted as missing from the repo.
Platforms (`platforms/claude_code.py`, `platforms/gh_cli.py`) are described as pure-data row files with no def/class, proving a file-discovered platform registry pattern.
Skills and contexts are described as file-discovered registries with constitutions and data-only modules.

A dedicated 'Notable Gaps / Stale / Incomplete / Surprises' section records stale docs, missing systemd units, unconfirmed model IDs, shared ports, manual services, and other operational inconsistencies.
standouts: The `company` CLI is framed as the ONE console for the entire running system, with every subcommand mapped to its implementation module.
`ops/services.json` is the single registry of truth for all services, and `ops/cli/gpu.py` is the single shared VRAM authority.
The boot/runbook section gives a concrete 6-step minimal post-reboot sequence and explicitly notes what persists vs what is lost.
The file does not hide defects: it has a large 'Gaps / Stale / Incomplete / Surprises' section that treats broken or unfinished things as first-class landscape data.
conventions: One source of truth: a single JSON registry (`services.json`) drives CLI, systemd, and resource management.
Fail loud / honest over complete: missing files, malformed catalogs, and over-budget starts are refused or surfaced rather than silently patched.
File-discovered declarations: platforms, skills, contexts, and node-types are declared in data-only Python modules discovered by filename/id rules.
Type-views: session, board, and clone are presented as distinct conceptual surfaces with their own CLI subcommands.
Registry-driven systemd units: vLLM services are launched via `ops/serve_model.sh` parameterized by service key from `services.json`.
Read-only extraction with explicit freshness: the file is dated 2026-06-21 and repeatedly marks sections as 'not fully read' rather than fabricating detail.
Operational transparency: shared ports, manual services, eviction order, and boot dependencies are documented as observable facts.
concerns: The landscape is a dated snapshot (2026-06-21) and several subsections are explicitly marked 'not fully read', so its coverage is uneven.
It catalogs many known gaps and stale docs but does not resolve them, which may leave readers with a long to-do list and no prioritization.
Despite describing a registry-driven system, this landscape file itself is prose/markdown rather than machine-readable structured data.
It mixes verified operational facts with inferred gaps and surprises, so readers must distinguish observed state from flagged uncertainty.
No code symbols are extracted from this file; it is purely interpretive/documentation content.
notes: A dense, unfiltered ops field guide that maps every service, CLI command, systemd unit, runbook, and known defect in one markdown snapshot.
questions: Which callers actually use `ops/commit_queue.py` versus still performing direct `git commit`?
What is the exact implementation of `company coherence` and why is it missing from `ops/cli/README.md`?

What are the contents of the unread docs and skills (`docs/claude-code-interconnection/README.md`, `REFERENCE.md`, `ROADMAP.md`, `skills/inversion_query.py`, `skills/map_reduce_composition.py`, `skills/patterned_visibility.py`)?
Are the five voice trial engine systemd units actually present in `~/.config/systemd/user/`, and why have they not been canonicalized into `ops/systemd/`?
Why is there no `company routine` CLI verb even though generated routine systemd units exist?
How exactly does `ops/cli/capabilities.py` join rows from `ops/model_capabilities.json` with live service state and VRAM budgets?
purpose_vs_actual: The stated purpose—an exhaustive, unfiltered, read-only landscape of the ops area—matches the actual content: it enumerates CLI, registry, scripts, units, docs, skills, contexts, and explicitly records gaps/stale items without trying to fix them.
apparent_themes: one console / one authority per concern
fail loud and honest over complete
read-only extraction as a valid knowledge layer
intention_signals: The file is trying to create a single readable map of the entire ops substrate so a human or agent can understand what exists, how it is operated, and what is broken or incomplete without reading every source file; it treats gaps and stale docs as first-class data rather than hiding them.
novelty: It is an interpretive, gap-aware landscape layer that sits above code: it does not just list files but explains their role in the operational control surface, names the 'ONE' authorities (CLI, registry, VRAM manager), and explicitly inventories stale docs and missing units as part of the system's self-model.
contribution: It contributes a human-readable, single-file operational index that maps `company` CLI commands to their implementation modules, lists every service with port/unit/VRAM metadata, documents boot/runbook sequences, and records known gaps/stale items—a navigational and onboarding artifact for the ops substrate.
summary_for_embedding: AREA-ops.md is an exhaustive read-only landscape inventory of the Company's ops control surface, service registry, systemd units, runbooks, scripts, platforms, docs, skills, contexts, and known gaps.
intention_for_embedding: It was created to give a single unfiltered map of the operational substrate and its defects so operators and agents can orient themselves without reading every source file.
suggested_fields: operational_status (verified / stale / missing / manual) per service or doc
port_conflict_set for services sharing a port
manual_run_command for services without systemd units
owner / last_verified_by per gap or unit
